coredata.dc:title: Photobleach effect of multi-color emitting carbon dots for UV-light sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Jin Young
Rama Raju, Ganji Seeta
Hong, Woo Tae
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been developed as prospective nanomaterials for sensing applications owing to their cost-effectiveness, thermal stability, low toxicity, and unique luminescence properties. CDs sensors have been widely used for ion detection but rarely for light detection. Herein, fluorescent CDs from carbon black containing refilled blue ink has been prepared by a solvothermal reaction-assisted straightforward one-pot route under mild temperature conditions. The body color of the as-prepared CDs solution is light brown and shows blue emission under ultraviolet (UV)-light excitation. The UV-light detecting film has been developed by blending the CDs/poly(vinyl alcohol) solution and examined its sensitivity with the increase of UV-light irradiation time. The progression in the body (light-brown to purple) and emission (blue to orange) colors are distinguishable and demonstrates their feasibility in developing the high-performance CDs-based UV detectors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-color emitting
Solvothermal reaction
Photobleach
UV-light
Sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman spectrum, (c) TEM image and (d) high-resolution TEM image of the as-prepared CDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum and core-level XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s of the as-prepared CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of CDs (inset: photograph of the as-prepared CDs solution under daylight and 365 nm UV light). (b) Excitation-dependent PL spectra of CDs. (c) Optical images of the CDs solution excited at different wavelengths.

(a) Time-course fluorescence spectra. (b) Schematic diagram of a possible structural change of the as-prepared CDs through the photobleaching and deoxidation processes under prolonged UV irradiation. (c) CIE chromaticity coordinates of the CDs after the 365 nm UV light irradiation. (d) Photographs of the CDs solution under the daylight and 365 nm UV light after the 365 nm UV light irradiation. (e) Pixel profiles of the CDs solution under the 365 nm UV irradiation corresponding to (c).
Photographs of CDs/PVA films under the daylight (a) and 365 nm UV irradiation (b) after the 365 nm UV light irradiation from 0 to 1080 min. (the numbers in the figure represent minutes).

head.ce:title: Photobleach effect of multi-color emitting carbon dots for UV-light sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48547, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48547, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy and Materials Engineering, Dongguk University-Seoul, Seoul 04620, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy and Materials Engineering, Dongguk University-Seoul, Seoul 04620, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Marine-Bionics Convergence Technology Center, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Marine-Bionics Convergence Technology Center, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been developed as prospective nanomaterials for sensing applications owing to their cost-effectiveness, thermal stability, low toxicity, and unique luminescence properties. CDs sensors have been widely used for ion detection but rarely for light detection. Herein, fluorescent CDs from carbon black containing refilled blue ink has been prepared by a solvothermal reaction-assisted straightforward one-pot route under mild temperature conditions. The body color of the as-prepared CDs solution is light brown and shows blue emission under ultraviolet (UV)-light excitation. The UV-light detecting film has been developed by blending the CDs/poly(vinyl alcohol) solution and examined its sensitivity with the increase of UV-light irradiation time. The progression in the body (light-brown to purple) and emission (blue to orange) colors are distinguishable and demonstrates their feasibility in developing the high-performance CDs-based UV detectors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) with less than 10 nm in size are defined as zero-dimensional nanocarbons. These CDs consist of sp2/sp3 carbon skeletons with rich surface functional groups/polymer chains [1–5]. In contrast to their well-known counter species such as fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and graphene, CDs have unique color-tunable fluorescence properties and allow facile functionalization [6]. Additionally, CDs exhibit low toxicity, good water dispersibility, high thermal stability, and chemical inertness [7–9]. These versatile properties make them potential nanomaterials for applications in various fields such as biolabeling, optical sensing, anti-counterfeiting, light-emitting diodes, drug delivery, biosensing, bio-imaging, and photothermal therapy [10–17].

As mentioned above, CDs are widely used in fluorescence sensing applications. Fluorescent sensor systems are available in two forms: solution-phase and solid-phase. CDs-based solution-phase sensors can sense various analytes such as pH, Cu2+, Fe3+, and I- [18–21]. In contrast, CD-based solid-phase sensors are used in fingerprint, test paper, and electrospun nanofibrous film sensing. However, to the best of our knowledge there are no reports on CD-based sensors for detection of ultraviolet (UV) rays. The UV rays of the sun interact with skin proteins and convert them into vitamin D3 [22,23]. Vitamin D3 is the active form of Vitamin D, which promotes the absorption of calcium and phosphorus in the intestine. Vitamin D helps in maintaining adequate blood levels of calcium and phosphorus, which are essential for bone mineralization. It also plays a key role in other functions of the body, including regulation of inflammation and immunity. When the skin is exposed to sunlight, it produces Vitamin D; however, excessive exposure to UV rays damages the cellular DNA of the skin, leading to skin cancer. Clothes, sunscreens, hats, and sunglasses can protect the skin from overexposure to UV rays. UV-light rays are essential for the body and should not be excessive, thus it is an ultimate requirement to sense or detect UV-light ray. But it is not easy to know how much and how strong UV rays are irradiated in our daily life.

To date, semiconductors, particularly those with wide band gap, such as TiO2, GaN, ZnO, and SiC, have been utilized for UV detectors because large-band-gap semiconductors display good wavelength sensitivity and rapid photo-response [24–28]. Because of the low-intensity of UV light rays, photodetectors based on wide-band-gap semiconductors require amplifiers and sophisticated measurement systems, in addition to complex assembly processes and high production costs [28]. Therefore, there is a need to develop a cost-effective UV detector that does not require any additional support or sophisticated measurement systems.

Herein, we introduce a cost-effective UV detector that uses CDs. Industrial-scale fluorescent CDs were prepared from carbon black using a facile one-pot solvothermal route at mild temperatures. Carbon black was derived from refilled blue ink. The fluorescence intensity ratios of the CDs were observed upon irradiation with UV light rays. We demonstrated that the color of the CDs solution and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs could be changed by UV light irradiation. Interestingly, the CDs exhibited unique fluorescence properties, indicating their potential for use in UV detectors.

The refilled-ink-derived CDs were synthesized using the solvothermal method with urea as the nitrogen source and dimethylformamide (DMF) as the solvent. Refilled blue ink is a universal ink used in Canon, Samsung, and HP inkjet printers (Jio Tech Ink Co. Ltd., Korea). To synthesize CDs, 44 ml of refilled blue ink and 11 g of urea were mixed with 450 ml of DMF. Then, the mixed solution was sealed in a 1 L Teflon autoclave and heated to 200 °C with a ramp time of 2 h. This temperature was maintained for 20 h. Magnetic stirring was used up to a reaction temperature of 200 °C and then stopped. After the completion of the reaction, the samples were naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained CDs solution was used for further experiments.

A transparent poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) solution was prepared by dissolving 2 g of PVA (with an average molecular weight of 22000, and purity grade of 500) in 30 ml deionized water under continuous magnetic stirring at 90 °C. The PVA solution was mixed with the as-prepared CDs solution in the volume ratio of 1:1 and stirred to obtain a homogeneous solution. The resultant CDs/PVA solution was poured into a laboratory dish and dried at ambient temperature in a dark room for two days to obtain CD film.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a PANalytical X’PERT PRO MPD X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (scanning rate of 10° min−1). Raman spectrum was obtained using an NRS-5100 Micro Raman spectrometer (JASCO) with a 532 nm laser source. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded using a JEOL JEM-F200 field emission transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained using an FT 4100 spectrometer (JASCO) in the range 640–4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe spectrometer. The UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of the CDs solution was obtained using a V670 UV–vis spectrophotometer (JASCO). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CDs were recorded using an FP-8500 spectrofluorometer (JASCO). The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was measured using an FP-8500 spectrofluorometer with a 120 mm integrating sphere. The fluorescence decay time was measured using a Fluorolog-QM-75–22 system (HORIBA Scientific) with a 375 nm laser.

The CDs were prepared by a simple one-step solvothermal method at 200 °C, and no further modification steps were used. The XRD pattern of the CDs (Fig. 1 a) displays a broad (002) plane broad diffraction peak centered at approximately 21.7°, indicating the amorphous nature of the CDs [29]. The amorphous nature is attributed to the presence of a large number of carbon atoms in a disordered manner with a graphitic interlayer spacing of 0.31 nm. The Raman spectrum of the as-prepared CDs (Fig. 1b) shows the characteristic D band at 1359 cm−1 (corresponding to out-of-plane vibrations due to structural defects) and G band at 1581 cm−1 (corresponding to in-plane vibrations of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms). The broad D band indicates increased sp3 hybridization between carbon–carbon, carbon–oxygen, epoxy-hydroxyl groups, etc. [30]. The narrow G band is caused by the stretching of sp2 hybridized CC bonds in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [31]. The degree of disorder D- to- G band intensity ratio (ID/IG) is approximately 1.8; the increased defect density is attributed to oxygen-containing surface functional groups [32,33]. The morphology of the as-prepared CDs was examined by TEM, and the images are shown in Fig. 1c. The TEM images reveal that the as-prepared CDs are monodispersed with spherical morphology and an average size of 5.1 ± 0.8 nm. The HRTEM image displays lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.31 nm, which matches the (002) interplanar distance in graphitic carbon [21,34].

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 a) was used to determine the surface functional groups and structural composition of the CDs. The broad absorption band in the FTIR spectrum centered at approximately 3450 cm−1 confirms the presence of –OH groups in the as-prepared CDs. This indicates that the CDs have good hydrophobicity [35]. The bands centered at 2850 and 2950 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibrations of –CH2 and –CH3, respectively. The intense peak at approximately 1654 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CN/CO [36]. The bands centered at 1504, 1441, 1386, 1255, and 1093 cm−1 originate from CC, CH, CO, and COC stretching vibrations [19,20,37,38]. These results confirm that the CDs consist of sp2 conjugated oxygen- and nitrogen-containing surface functional groups. These results were corroborated by the XPS survey spectra.

Fig. S1 shows the XPS survey scan spectrum with C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks located at 284.6, 400.0, and 531.0 eV. High-resolution XPS spectra were recorded to study the surface chemical composition of the CDs. The high-resolution C1s peak (Fig. 2b) was deconvoluted into four peaks, which confirmed the bonding of carbon with N, O, and OH group. The deconvoluted peaks centered at 284.9, 285.6, 286.2 and 287.6 eV are attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO, and COH, respectively [39]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows peaks centered at 399.6, 400.0, and 401.9 eV corresponding to pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and amino N, respectively [40]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum also confirms the presence of carbon–oxygen bonds; the deconvoluted peaks at 531.6 and 532.5 eV are assigned to CO and COH/COC, respectively [41]. The XPS results are in good agreement with the FTIR results.

To analyze the fluorescence properties of the as-prepared CDs, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded; the results are shown in Fig. 3 a. The as-prepared CDs exhibit peaks centered at 269 and 313 nm in the absorption spectrum which are attributed to the π–π* transitions of aromatic CC and n–π* transition of CO bonds, respectively [42,43]. The excitation and fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared CDs are distinct and well-defined with peaks centered at 372 and 446 nm, respectively.

To confirm the emission center of the as-prepared CDs, excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra were obtained by changing the excitation wavelength from 340 to 500 nm at intervals of 10 nm, as shown in Fig. 3b. Although many researchers have reported both excitation-dependent and excitation-independent fluorescence phenomena of CDs, our CDs exhibited completely different properties, which has not been reported so far [44–47]. The excitation-dependent and excitation-independent fluorescence spectra show two emission centers. One is blue-dominated and excitation-dependent emission, where the emission centers shift when the excitation wavelength changes from 340 to 420 nm. This phenomenon is attributed to the association of different energy levels with different functional groups forming ‘‘surface states’’ [48]. However, when the excitation wavelength is between 420 and 500 nm, there is no considerable shift in the emission centers. This indicates excitation-independent characteristics and is caused by the uniform and well-yielded CDs surfaces. Fig. 3c shows the optical images at different excitation wavelengths, which reveal the multicolor emission properties of the CDs.

The optical sensing performance of the as-prepared CDs was evaluated by examining the color changes in the CD solution upon irradiation with 365 nm UV light. The color changes after different durations of irradiation were recorded, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The time-course fluorescence spectra of the CDs (Fig. 4a) exhibit three emission centers at 446, 520, and 583 nm, which may be attributed to molecular fluorophores, surface state of the CDs, and intrinsic state of the carbogenic core, respectively [49]. The intensities of the emission bands at 446 and 520 nm decrease gradually with increase in UV-light irradiation time, whereas that of the emission band at 583 nm significantly increases (Fig. 4a). We also examined the excitation properties of the as-prepared CDs and UV light-irradiated CDs (after 240 min of irradiation) by monitoring the peaks at 446, 520, and 583 nm, as shown in Fig. S2. The as-prepared CDs show dominant excitation as compared to 365 nm UV-light irradiated CDs when the emission wavelength monitored at 446 and 520 nm, whereas the dominant excitation of UV-light irradiated CDs over the as-prepared CDs is confirmed by the emission peak at 583 nm. This is because the molecular fluorophores and surface state of the CDs exhibit emissions under prolonged UV light irradiation have been photobleached, and the intrinsic state of the inner carbogenic core emission is enhanced owing to the surface deoxidation of the CDs [49]. These results indicate that energy transfer in CDs occurs from the wavelength of 372 to 491 nm energy level and 491 to 561 nm energy level, resulting in intense emission at 583 nm. With increase in the duration of UV light irradiation of CDs, the energy transfer process is limited between the above-mentioned energy levels because the UV irradiation causes deterioration of the 372 and 491 nm energy bands. This is because the as-prepared CDs consist of an oxidized carbonized core (CC) attached with several molecular fluorophores (MFs). As shown in Fig. 4b, CC, surface oxygen, and MFs exhibit their own emission centers. It is known that MFs and surface oxygen develop over CC, indicating that the optical transition states of MFs and surface oxygen are higher than those of the CC. Therefore, with increase in the UV-light irradiation time, the attached MFs and surface oxygen atoms are easily photobleached and deoxidized, altering the creation and recombination of charge carriers in the CDs. Owing to the diminishing MFs and surface oxygen, the related energy levels also decrease, which is clearly observed in the absorption and fluorescence spectra. The fluorescence decays of as-prepared CDs and UV light-irradiated CDs were measured by monitoring the emission wavelength at 446, 520, and 583 nm at 372 nm laser excitation wavelength, as shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1.The fluorescence decay curves (Fig. S3a) of the as-prepared CDs and UV light-irradiated CDs fit well with the double exponential function and exhibited the average lifetimes of approximately 2.47 and 2.92 ns, respectively. It can be seen that the lifetimes of as-prepared CDs at the emission of 446 nm are shorter in comparison with that of the UV light-irradiated CDs due to different recombination mechanisms as explained above. However, lifetimes at 520 and 583 nm for UV light-irradiated CDs are close to the as-prepared CDs. Consequently, to establish the luminescence efficiency for the as-prepared CDs under the excitation wavelengths of 372 nm and the prolonged UV light irradiated CDs under the excitation wavelengths of 561 nm, internal quantum efficiency (IQE), absorption rate (A) and external quantum efficiency (EQE) of the CDs were measured with the help of integrating sphere technique. The A, EQE and IQE values of the as-prepared CDs was summarized in Table S1. The EQE of the CDs (Table S1) increases from 1.41 to 33.75% after prolonged UV light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4c, the Commission Internationale de l'Elcairage chromaticity coordinates red-shift from (0.216, 0.270) to (0.396, 0.330) with increase in the UV-light irradiation time. Interestingly, a change in the color of the CD solution is visible to the naked eye after the solution is illuminated with 365 nm UV light, as shown in Fig. 4d. These observations are clear in the pixel profiles in Fig. 4e. The blue value decreases and the red value increases with increase in the exposure time to 365 nm UV light.

To confirm the applicability of the as-prepared CDs as UV detector films, CDs/poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) composite films were fabricated. PVA was utilized as the host matrix for the CDs owing to its high transparency. When water and PVA solution were mixed with the CDs solution synthesized using DMF, the color of the CDs/PVA film turned white, indicating bleaching between the PVA solution and DMF. Fig. 5 a shows the optical images of the CDs/PVA films under the daylight after irradiation with 365 nm UV light (intensity: 70 lx) as a function of the irradiation time. As shown in Fig. 5a, the body color of the as-prepared CDs/PVA film is white, which changes to purple as the irradiation time increases. Fig. 5b shows the fluorescent optical images of the CDs/PVA film corresponding to Fig. 5a under a 365 nm UV lamp. The emission color of the film changes from blue to orange depending on the UV light irradiation time. The time-course fluorescence spectra of the CDs/PVA film is shown in Fig. S4. The fluorescence spectra exhibit three emission centers at around 440 and 520 and 583 nm, which may be attributed to molecular fluorophores, surface state of the CDs, and intrinsic state of the carbogenic core, respectively [49]. The intensities of the emission bands at around 440 and 520 nm decreased gradually with increase of the UV-light irradiation time, whereas the emission intensity at 583 nm increases significantly. Fig. S5a and b show the photographs of the CDs/PVA film used as a golf ball marker before and after playing golf. After 4 h of playing golf, the body color of the CDs/PVA film changes from white to purple; this is similar to the color of the film irradiated with UV light of 70 lx for 270 min (Fig. 5a). These results demonstrate that CDs are sensitive to UV light and are promising for use as UV detectors.

In summary, we synthesized CDs via a solvothermal reaction using refilled blue ink for UV detection. The as-prepared CDs solution showed blue emission, whereas the UV light-irradiated CDs solution exhibited orange emission. The synthesized CDs demonstrated excitation-dependent and excitation-independent fluorescence owing to the varied densities of MFs and surface oxygen contents over the CCs of the CDs. The fluorescence from the surface MFs and surface states exhibited low photostability, whereas the CCs displayed higher photostability under prolonged UV light irradiation. The CDs/PVA film as an UV light detector showed white in body color and blue in emission color before UV light irradiation. After UV light irradiation, the body and emission colors of the film changed to purple and orange, respectively according to the duration of exposure. The obtained results indicate that these CDs can be used to determine the amount, intensity, and duration of UV light exposure in daily life. We believe that the findings of this study will provide valuable insights for further research on the design of CDs and optimization of devices based on them. This will enable the fabrication of CDs-based UV detectors with considerably improved performance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (Grant No. NRF-2020R1I1A1A01052352). This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2021R1A6A3A01088346). This work was also supported by an NRF grant (Grant No. 2020R1A2C400219311) funded by the Korean government (MSIT; Ministry of Science and ICT).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.142643.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Y. Song, X. Zhao, J. Shao, J. Zhang, B. Yang, The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): Current state and future perspective, Nano Res. 8 (2015), 355–381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.T. Zheng, A. Ananthanaratanan, K.Q. Luo, P. Chen, Glowing Graphene Quantum Dots and Carbon Dots: Properties, Syntheses, and Biological Applications, Small 11 (2015), 1620–1636.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, and bioimaging application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.C. Ray, A. Saha, N.R. Jana, R.C. Sarkar, Fluorescent Carbon Nanoparticles: Synthesis, Characterization, and Bioimaging Application, J. Phys. Chem. C 113 (2009), 18546-18551.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toward high-efficient red emissive carbon dots: facile preparation, unique properties, and applications as multifunctional theranostic agents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sun, L. Zhang, K. Jiang, A. Wu, H. Lin, Toward high-efficient red emissive carbon dots: facile preparation, unique properties, and applications as multifunctional theranostic agents, Chem. Mater. 28 (2016), 8659-8668.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gram-scale synthesis of single-crystalline graphene quantum dots with superior optical properties

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, S. Sun, L. Zhang, Y. Lu, A. Wu, C. Cai, H. Lin, Red, Green, and Blue Luminescence by Carbon Dots: Full-Color Emission Tuning and Multicolor Cellular Imaging, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 54(2015), 5360–5363.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots with selective antibacterial activity against Porphyromonas gingivalis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-soluble and phosphorus-containing carbon dots with strong green fluorescence for cell labeling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Wang, Y. Li, L. Cheng, Z. Cao, W. Liu, Water-soluble and phosphorus-containing carbon dots with strong green fluorescence for cell labeling, J. Mater. Chem. B 2 (2014), 46-48.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Y. Lim, W. Shen, Z. Gao, Carbon Quantum Dots and Their Applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 44 (2015), 362−381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly luminescent carbon nanodots by microwave-assisted pyrolysis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid producesa ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Zhu, Q. Qu, X. Shao, B. Kong, Y. Tian, Carbon-Dot-Based Dual-Emission Nanohybrid Producesa Ratiometric Fluorescent Sensor for In Vivo Imaging of Cellular Copper Ions, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 51 (2012), 7185 –7189.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile, quick, and gram-scale synthesis of ultralong-lifetime room-temperature-phosphorescent carbon dots by microwave irradiation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, Y. Wang, X. Gao, C. Cai, H. Lin, Facile, Quick, and Gram-Scale Synthesis of Ultralong-Lifetime Room-Temperature-Phosphorescent Carbon Dots by Microwave Irradiation, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 57 (2018), 6216–6220.
S. Bhattacharya, R.S. Phatake, S.N. Barnea, N. Zerby, J. Zhu, R. Shikler, N.G. Lemcoff, E. Jelinek, Fluorescent Self-Healing Carbon Dot/Polymer Gels, ACS Nano 13 (2) (2019), 1433–1442.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly biocompatible carbon nanodots for simultaneous bioimaging and targeted photodynamic therapy in vitro and in vivo

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Jia, J. Li, E. Wang, One-pot green synthesis of optically pH-sensitive carbon dots with upconversion luminescence, Nanoscale 4 (2014), 5572-5575.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) Ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Liu, J. Tian, L. Wang, Y. Zhang, X. Qin, Y. Luo, A. M. Asiri, A. O. Al-Youbi, X. Sun, Hydrothermal Treatment of Grass: A Low-Cost, Green Route to Nitrogen-Doped, Carbon-Rich, Photoluminescent Polymer Nanodots as an Effective Fluorescent Sensing Platform for Label-Free Detection of Cu(II) Ions, Adv. Mater. 24 (2012), 2037–2041.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of iodide via direct fluorescence quenching at nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot fluorophores
